summary: Gruber examines the rumored switch to USB-C on the iPhone 15 lineup, exploring the likely differentiation between Pro and non-Pro models in data transfer speeds. He explains why selling a Thunderbolt 4 cable separately makes sense given their expense, and why faster transfer speeds matter most for video workflows. His central prediction is that the switch will trigger a consumer backlash from normal users who don't care about USB-C standardization and just see a forced cable replacement — a reaction he believes USB-C proponents are underestimating.
key_points: Thunderbolt 4 cables are inherently expensive ($30-$159), making it reasonable for Apple to sell them separately rather than including one with every iPhone

The speed gap between USB 2.0 (480 Mbps) on non-Pro and Thunderbolt 4 (40 Gbps) on Pro models — an 80x difference behind identical-looking connectors — perfectly illustrates USB-C's confusing standards problem
Apple likely introduced MagSafe three years before the USB-C switch deliberately, giving users time to accumulate wireless charging setups before disrupting their Lightning cables
The vast majority of iPhone users will see the USB-C switch not as a welcome standardization but as a money grab forcing them to replace billions of existing Lightning cables
Pro-quality 4K video shooting on iPhones has been hamstrung by consumer-level USB 2.0 transfer speeds since 2015 — an eight-year incongruity finally being resolved
money_quotes: Heretofore, iPhones have offered pro-quality video cameras limited to decidedly consumer-level data transfer rates.

The fact that some of these phones might support only USB 2 speeds (480 Mbps), while others will support up to Thunderbolt 4 (40 Gbps — roughly 80× faster), yet all the ports and cable connectors will look the same to the naked eye, only goes to show just how confusing 'USB-C' is.

I think there's going to be a backlash that most USB-C proponents don't see coming, premised on accusations that this switch is a money grab from Apple to get people to replace all their Lightning cables.
At a glance USB-C doesn't look any better than and barely different from Lightning.

Typical users put more value on being able to just keep using the old cables they already have — at their bedsides, in their kitchens, on their desks, in their cars, in their backpacks and purses — than reducing the number of different types of cables they need in their lives.

I don't know how many people are going to be irritated, if not downright angry, about this switch, but it's going to be a lot more people than most 'the iPhone should have already switched to USB-C' proponents expect.
Me, I'll gladly take the vastly-improved data transfer speeds. But I won't be the one answering the phone at 1-800-MY-APPLE.
I think this is the single biggest reason Apple has waited so long to switch the iPhone to USB-C.
key_insight: The tech-savvy celebration of USB-C standardization will be drowned out by a much larger consumer backlash from ordinary iPhone users who see only forced cable obsolescence, not progress.
